We know how critical it is to have a clear, unified view of project financials. That's why we’ve introduced the company code currency into the Project Cost report. Now, you can see a consolidated view of all your key cost figures including planned costs, actuals, commitments, and even project stock costs – all in a single, consistent currency. This provides a much clearer picture of your project's overall financial health and variance.

There is one important prerequisite to keep in mind: for the report to display in the company code currency, all the projects and WBS elements you select for that report must share the same company code. This ensures data integrity and accurate reporting. For a more detailed guide on this feature, I encourage you to check out the documentation on the SAP Help Portal.

Second, we’ve Improved system usability by segregating the profitability segment from settlement rules for clearer allocation and analysis. What does this mean for you? In the past, assigning profitability might have required you to set up a settlement rule. Now, for projects with revenue, you can assign the profitability segment directly to a billing WBS element using the "Assign" function. This is a much more direct and intuitive workflow. It saves you steps, simplifies the allocation process, and makes your analysis clearer and more efficient. As with all our features, you can find comprehensive details on this enhancement in the SAP Help Portal.

Finally, and this is something I’m personally very excited about, we are going beyond system features to build a stronger community. We are officially introducing the new CIP community – a collaborative space designed for our customers, partners, and SAP experts to connect. The goal is to build a powerful network where we can all share ideas, discuss best practices, and co-innovate on the future of enterprise and collaborative project scenarios. This is your platform to influence the direction of our products and learn from your peers.

If you are interested in joining this community and helping shape the future of project management at SAP, please reach out to my colleagues, Anke Junker anke.junker@sap.com or Varsha Chetan Manu varsha.chetan.manu@sap.com, to get involved.


The SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release for EPPM is all about making your project management more connected, automated, and intelligent. From ensuring a single source of truth to simplifying daily tasks for every project role, these innovations are designed to deliver tangible business value and set you up for success.

Thank you for your time, and I look forward to seeing how you leverage these new capabilities.

 

 

📽️ Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series!

From January 12-16, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find the recording and presentation for Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management as well as 21 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand – anywhere, anytime.

 

🔎Find all our assets

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: Developing custom Agent Protocol for SAP based on A2A
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

Last months I deep dive in topics like MCP, A2A and other related.
Also I found that there is AgentPaymentProtocol for a different use cases about payment activities.

So maybe creating a custom Protocol for specific area, usecases, products will give an advantage to it's creators?
As SAP Strategy is AI <-> Data <-> Apps and base on the recent AI related topic I checked one idea settled in my mind.

Is it possible to create/develop a specific protocol for SAP ecosystem - AgentResourcePlanningProtocol (AERPP or AERP).

Most probably using A2A as a good base will help us a lot!

Maybe this way we can scale SAP Agent development with seamless integration among SAP portfolio.
I already start with some investigation and researching and I will be very happy if someone else find this idea as interesting and reasonable.

If someone is curious feel free to contact me! 🙂

Thanks for your time!
Kristiyan

entry.title: Building an Email-Based SAP Sales Order Automation with n8n & AI Function Calling
entry.content:
Building an Email-Based SAP Sales Order Assistant with AI Function Calling

The Problem That Started This

A few weeks ago, I was watching a colleague struggle with a simple request: "Can you check if order 10850 shipped?" They had to VPN in, open SAP GUI, navigate to VA03, enter the order number, and finally get the answer. For one question. That took 3 minutes.

I thought - what if they could just send an email and get an answer back?

Why Not Just Use Rules or Keywords?

My first instinct was simple pattern matching. Look for "create order" then run the create flow. Look for "check status" then run the query flow. Easy, right?

Then I looked at actual emails people send:

  • "Hey, can you set up an order for 17100003? They need 5 of TG11 and 10 of TG12"
  • "pls make SO for cust 17100003 - TG11 x5, TG12 x10"
  • "Customer 17100003 wants to place an order. Materials: TG11 (5 units), TG12 (10 units)"

Same request, completely different wording. Building regex patterns for every variation? That's a maintenance nightmare waiting to happen. And what about typos, abbreviations, or when someone writes in a mix of languages?

This is exactly where AI shines - understanding intent regardless of how it's expressed.

Why Function Calling Instead of Free-Form AI?

I didn't want the AI making up SAP field names or guessing at API structures. That's a recipe for errors. Function calling gives you the best of both worlds:

  • The AI handles the messy, unpredictable part (understanding what the user wants)
  • The code handles the precise, structured part (calling SAP correctly)

You define exactly what functions exist and what parameters they accept. The AI picks the right function and extracts the right values. No hallucinated field names, no invented API calls.

The Architecture

Four components working together:

  1. Microsoft Outlook - Triggers when an email arrives
  2. LLM with Function Calling - Interprets the email, picks the operation
  3. n8n - Orchestrates everything, handles data transformation
  4. SAP S/4HANA OData APIs - Does the actual work

 

Setting Up n8n with Docker (The Network Gotchas)

I run n8n locally in Docker, and connecting it to an on-premise SAP system over VPN was... an adventure. Here's what I learned.

The Basic Setup

docker run -it --rm \
  --name n8n \
  -p 5678:5678 \
  --add-host=host.docker.internal:host-gateway \
  -v n8n_data:/home/node/.n8n \
  docker.n8n.io/n8nio/n8n

That --add-host=host.docker.internal:host-gateway flag is crucial. It lets containers reference your host machine's network. Without it, your container is isolated and can't see anything your host can see - including VPN connections.

The VPN Problem

Here's the thing: when you connect to a VPN on your Mac, the VPN routes are available to your host machine, but Docker containers live in their own network namespace. They can't directly reach 172.16.0.11:44300 even though your browser can.

You might think "just use --network host" - but on macOS, that doesn't work the way you'd expect. Docker Desktop on Mac runs containers in a Linux VM, so --network host gives you the VM's network, not your Mac's network.

The socat Bridge Solution

The workaround is to run a TCP forwarder on your host that bridges the connection:

socat TCP-LISTEN:15001,fork,reuseaddr TCP:172.16.0.11:44300

This listens on port 15001 on your Mac and forwards traffic to the SAP server at 172.16.0.11:44300. Now from inside Docker, you can reach SAP at:

https://host.docker.internal:15001/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SALES_ORDER_SRV/A_SalesOrder

The flow is: Docker container → host.docker.internal:15001 → socat on your Mac → VPN → SAP server

Keep the socat command running in a terminal while you're working. Kill it when you're done.

Quick Tip: If you're getting "connection refused" errors, check that:

  1. Your VPN is connected
  2. socat is running
  3. You're using host.docker.internal, not localhost or the direct IP

The Functions I Defined

Three operations cover most daily requests:

get_sales_orders

"Show me recent orders," "What's the status of order X?"

Parameters: $top, $filter, $orderby

create_sales_order

"Create an order for customer X with these items"

Parameters: SoldToParty, items[] (Material, RequestedQuantity), plus optional fields

update_sales_order

"Change the PO reference on order 10904"

Parameters: SalesOrder (required), plus any fields to update

When an email comes in, the AI returns structured JSON:

{
  "function": "create_sales_order",
  "arguments": {
    "SoldToParty": "17100003",
    "items": [
      {"Material": "TG11", "RequestedQuantity": "5"},
      {"Material": "TG12", "RequestedQuantity": "10"}
    ]
  }
}

Clean, predictable, easy to work with.

The CSRF Token Headache

I have to mention this because it cost me hours. SAP OData APIs require CSRF tokens for write operations. Sounds simple - fetch a token, send it with your POST. But here's what the documentation doesn't make obvious:

The token is tied to your session cookies. You need to:

  1. GET request with x-csrf-token: fetch header
  2. Capture the token AND the SAP_SESSIONID cookie from the response
  3. Send BOTH with your write request

I kept getting "CSRF token validation failed" with a perfectly valid token. Turns out I was missing the session cookie. The token alone is useless without it.

Another gotcha in n8n

The cookie header comes back with extra metadata like path=/; secure; HttpOnly. If you pass that whole string in your Cookie header, you'll get "Invalid character in header content." You need to extract just the cookie value:

// In an Edit Fields node, clean the cookie
$json.headers['set-cookie'][0].split(';')[0] + '; ' + 
$json.headers['set-cookie'][1].split(';')[0]

This gives you a clean sap-usercontext=sap-client=100; SAP_SESSIONID_S4H_100=abc123... that SAP will accept.

 

Data Transformation for Deep Insert

One more SAP quirk: when creating an order with line items, you can't just send an items array. SAP expects a nested structure called to_Item.results. In n8n's Code node:

const args = JSON.parse(
  $input.first().json.choices[0].message.tool_calls[0].function.arguments
);
const body = { ...args };

if (body.items) {
  body.to_Item = { results: body.items };
  delete body.items;
}

return [{ json: { sapBody: body } }];

This transforms the AI's clean output into SAP's expected format.

 

A Real Example

Input Email

Hello, please create a sales order with the following details:
Customer: 17100003
Sales Org: 1710
Items: TG11 (qty 5), TG12 (qty 10)
Currency: USD

What Happens

  1. AI identifies this as create_sales_order
  2. Extracts: customer, items array, currency
  3. Workflow fetches CSRF token + session cookie
  4. Transforms items array to SAP's to_Item.results format
  5. POSTs to /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SALES_ORDER_SRV/A_SalesOrder
  6. SAP creates order, returns details

Response Email

The user never touched SAP. They just sent an email.

What's Next

The same pattern works for anything with an OData API:

  • Invoice queries
  • Delivery status checks
  • Material lookups
  • Customer master updates

Define the function, connect the endpoint, done.

The Takeaway

AI isn't replacing SAP knowledge here - it's translating between human language and system requirements. The business logic, the validations, the actual processing - that's all still SAP. The AI just makes it accessible to people who don't speak SAP.

And honestly? That's the kind of AI use case I find most valuable. Not replacing expertise, but removing friction.

entry.title: How AI Transforms Customer Experience (CX) in SAP Product Lines of Business
entry.content:
Introduction:

Customer experience (CX) has become the cornerstone of business success. In today’s digital economy, customers expect fast, personalized, and seamless interactions. Artificial Intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing how organizations deliver these experiences, and SAP’s Product Lines of Business (LoB) are leveraging AI to make this transformation possible.

1. Why AI Matters for Customer Service CX

  • Personalization at Scale: AI enables businesses to understand customer behavior and preferences, delivering tailored recommendations and solutions.
  • 24/7 Availability: AI-powered chatbots and virtual assistants ensure customers receive support anytime, anywhere.
  • Predictive Insights: AI can anticipate customer needs, reducing friction and improving satisfaction.
  • Efficiency and Cost Reduction: Automating repetitive tasks frees up human agents for complex issues, improving productivity.

2. AI Use Cases in Customer Service

  • Intelligent Chatbots: Handle FAQs, troubleshoot issues, and escalate complex cases.
  • Sentiment Analysis: Understand customer emotions in real-time to adapt responses.
  • Predictive Service: AI predicts potential issues before they occur, enabling proactive support.
  • Voice and Conversational AI: Enhance call center interactions with natural language processing.

3. How SAP LoB Integrates AI for CX

SAP’s LoBs (e.g., SAP Customer Experience, SAP Service Cloud, SAP Commerce Cloud) embed AI capabilities to deliver superior CX:

  • SAP Service Cloud: AI-driven ticket routing and automated case resolution.
  • SAP Commerce Cloud: Personalized product recommendations using machine learning.
  • SAP Sales Cloud: Intelligent lead scoring and next-best-action suggestions.
  • SAP Business AI: Embedded AI services across SAP applications for predictive analytics and automation.

4. Business Impact of AI in SAP CX

  • Improved Customer Satisfaction: Faster resolution and personalized engagement.
  • Operational Efficiency: Reduced handling time and optimized resource allocation.
  • Revenue Growth: Better upselling and cross-selling through AI-driven insights.
  • Scalability: AI enables businesses to handle growing customer demands without proportional cost increases.

5. Future Outlook

AI will continue to evolve with generative AI, conversational intelligence, and hyper-personalization. SAP’s commitment to embedding AI into its LoBs ensures businesses stay ahead in delivering exceptional CX.

Conclusion:

AI is not just a technology trend—it’s a strategic enabler for customer-centric organizations. By integrating AI into SAP’s Product LoBs, businesses can transform customer service into a competitive advantage.

entry.title: I built an AI Agent with LiteLLM, CrewAI and SAP-RPT-1: Here’s What Actually Worked
entry.content:
I hope everyone found their way into the new year and had some peaceful time over the holidays! Being back at work, I started to try out all the cool announcements from last year. One of them is SAP contributing to the third-party open-source Python library LiteLLM, where SAP now functions as an official LLM provider. That means you can now build AI Agents with the LiteLLM gateway and all the AI Agent frameworks that are compatible and provide your Generative AI Hub credentials to connect to LLMs via SAP's AI Foundation.

I tried out CrewAI first because it is such a powerful and widely used framework and I have been curious and willing to try it out for some time now!

First things first: What is LiteLLM?

LiteLLM is a library that provides a unified, provider-agnostic API for calling large language models (LLMs) and handling common tasks (completion, chat, streaming, multimodal inputs). It standardizes request/response handling and includes utilities that speed up integration with agent frameworks and tooling. Essentially it is a gateway between LLM providers and AI Agent frameworks.

That means you can use your Generative AI Hub credentials to build state of the art AI Agents with any of the models available through GenAI Hub and any of the AI Agent frameworks compatible with LiteLLM. This combination is extremely powerful because that means you can use LLMs hosted or managed by SAP (Mistral, Llama, Nvidia) and models from our partners such as Azure OpenAI, Amazon Bedrock (including Anthropic) and Gemini.

👉check all the available models here: https://me.sap.com/notes/3437766

👉And all the AI Agent frameworks including: CrewAI, LangGraph, PydanticAI, Google ADK, Strands SDK, OpenAI ADK, LlamaIndex, Microsoft Agents and many more. You can find a list with examples here: http://sap-contributions.github.io/litellm-agentic-examples/

How to get started

Now to get started you need an SAP AI Core instance with the extended plan (on SAP BTP), that means you have access to Generative AI Hub and all the LLMs available.

SAP BTP Cockpit, Instances and Subscriptions, SAP AI Core – extended plan

LiteLLM is directing calls to the LLMs through the orchestration service on Generative AI Hub. That means you do not need to deploy your models on SAP AI Core. You only need the out of the box deployment of the orchestration service. This way you can easily switch between all the models available via the orchestration service.

Screenshot from Model Library of SAP AI Launchpad showing models available via Orchestration Service.

 On a side note: you do not need SAP AI Launchpad as the UI to do any of this. You can also find your endpoints via API calls (more info: developer tutorial or documentation). or use the SAP Cloud SDK for AI.

Build your crew of agents

Now you only need to install Python. Once that is done you need to install the LiteLLM library and in my case also CrewAI:

pip install litellm crewai

Ok now we can actually start with building our AI Agent with CrewAI. As the name suggests you build a crew of agents that have a set of tools available to accomplish certain tasks. CrewAI uses tasks to bridge the gap between high-level goals and concrete agent actions, assigning specific objectives and expected outputs.

You also need to create a .env file to store the credentials from the Generative AI Hub service key. I also stored another variable RPT1_DEPLOYMENT_URL in that file, for the agent to use in the tool. Finally, it should look something like this:

AICORE_AUTH_URL="https://#######.ondemand.com/oauth/token"
AICORE_CLIENT_ID="sb-############-|aicore!b540"
AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET="#######"
AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP="default"
AICORE_BASE_URL="https://api.ai.prod.###.ml.hana.ondemand.com"
RPT1_DEPLOYMENT_URL="https://api.ai.prod.###.ml.hana.ondemand.com/v2/inference/deployments/###/predict"

Agents

CrewAI recommends storing your agent configuration in and agents.yaml file. Important are the parameters: role, goal, backstory and llm. The parameter llm defines which LLM provider you want to use. We want to use SAP as the provider and GPT-4o as the model (provider/model -> sap/gpt-4o). As already described above you can use any model here that is available via the orchestration service. {payload} is a variable that will be filled during runtime.

theft_crime_scene_investigator:
  role: >
    Theft Crime Scene Investigator
  goal: >
    Predict the missing values of stolen items using the SAP-RPT-1 model via the call_rpt1 tool use this payload {payload} as input.
  backstory: >
    An expert theft crime investigator specialised on predicting the value of stolen luxury goods.
  llm: sap/gpt-4o

Tasks

In the tasks.yaml you define the name of the task, the description, the expected output and which agent can do this task. {payload} is again the variable that will be filled during runtime.

inspection_task:
  description: >
    Analyze the theft crime scene and predict the missing values of stolen items using the SAP-RPT-1 model via the call_rpt1 tool. 
    Use this dict {payload} as input.
  expected_output: >
    JSON with predicted values for the stolen items.
  agent: theft_crime_scene_investigator

Tools

You can also define custom tools in Python and hand them over to your agents to use. The following tool for example gives the model access to the SAP-RPT-1 model (try it out here: rpt.cloud.sap).

from rpt1.call_rpt1 import RPT1Client

rpt1_client = RPT1Client()

@tool("call_rpt1")
def call_rpt1(payload: dict) -> str:
    """Function to call RPT-1 model via RPT1Client"""
    response = rpt1_client.post_request(json_payload=payload)
    if response.status_code == 200:
        return response.json()
    else:
        return f"Error: {response.status_code} - {response.text}"

If you want to know more about how to make the request to SAP-RPT-1 check out this code.

Building the Crew

You might wonder how this all comes together? Now you plug all those pieces together and build your crew. The crew needs at least one agent, but can have more, any tools you want the agent to use, you can also assign here. And each agent has at least one task.

from crewai import Agent, Crew, Task, Process
from crewai.project import CrewBase, agent, task, crew, tool

@CrewBase
class MurderMystery():
    """MurderMystery crew"""

    agents_config = "config/agents.yaml"
    tasks_config = 'config/tasks.yaml'

    
    def theft_crime_scene_investigator(self) -> Agent:
        return Agent(
            config=self.agents_config['theft_crime_scene_investigator'], 
            verbose=True,
            tools=[call_rpt1]
        )

    
    def inspection_task(self) -> Task:
        return Task(
            config=self.tasks_config['inspection_task'] # type: ignore[index]
        )

    @crew
    def crew(self) -> Crew:
        return Crew(
            agents=self.agents,  # Automatically collected by the  decorator
            tasks=self.tasks,    # Automatically collected by the  decorator.
            process=Process.sequential,
            verbose=True,
        )

Run the crew

murder_mystery = MurderMystery()
result = murder_mystery.crew().kickoff(inputs={'payload': payload})
print("\n📘 Result:\n", result)

 

Additional Infos

  • If you need any help in setting up your Python environment, check out the exercise 02 -Setup Python environment from my CodeJam.
  • Full code here: https://github.com/noravth/agentic-ai-codejam-collection
  • Documentation SAP and LiteLLM 

Happy Coding and feel free to reach out anytime!

entry.title: SAP AI Skills Gap Analysis: Are We Ready?
entry.content:
The SAP AI Skills Assessment results are out, and there's a fascinating exploration of the gaps and readiness across the globe. Since the initial publication, the dataset has grown from 68 to 141 responses spanning 10 countries. The study revalidates the 1.6-2.1 point gap between motivation and implementation across different geographies, identifying systemic challenges rather than measurement artifacts.

Key findings illustrate a persistent implementation challenge, an awareness trap, a governance thread, and a pronounced focus on S/4HANA. Though awareness and motivation are relatively high, actual implementation capabilities significantly lag behind, with only 6-17% of professionals globally ready to implement AI solutions.

The report suggests shifting the focus from awareness campaigns to hands-on enablement strategies to bridge this significant gap. To harness the high motivation levels, it recommends a detailed training and role-based learning path, along with practical implementation experiences. Visit the provided blog from Thomas Jenewein for an in-depth analysis and to view the complete results.

I am interested in hearing your thoughts on how organizations can effectively bridge the gap between high motivation for AI implementation and the actual skills required, ensuring that the enthusiasm for AI translates into real-world, impactful applications.  Please comment below.

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite: Intelligent Integration with AI Adapter
entry.content:
Overview

Enterprise integration is changing. The new AI Adapter for SAP Integration Suite makes it easier to add generative AI to integrations, workflows, and automations. This is a shift from just moving data between systems to building integrations that understand context and act more intelligently.

AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite(Cloud Integration), making it easier to use generative AI directly within integration flows on SAP BTP. It lets integration flow(iFlows) call AI or large language model services at runtime. It comes with two options:

  • AICore-GenAIHub: It connects to SAP AI Core through the GenAI Hub, giving access to multiple foundation models using a single, unified API.
  • Custom-OpenAI-Rest: It connects to external AI providers such as OpenAI through a simple REST based interface.

Architecture Overview

The AI Adapter is used as a receiver in an integration flow. SAP Integration Suite starts the call and sends the request to either SAP AI Core through the GenAI Hub or to OpenAI.

The AI service processes the request and sends the response back to SAP Integration Suite, where it is used in the next steps of the flow.

 

What can we do with it

With this AI adapter, you can run tasks like sentiment analysis, text classification, translation, or text summarisation directly inside an iFlow. It also allows real time data enrichment, for example analysing customer feedback before sending it to downstream systems. More advanced scenarios are possible too, such as AI driven routing decisions or smarter, automated error handling. When using the GenAI Hub, you can even switch between models without changing the integration logic.

What makes this important is how naturally AI fits into existing integration scenarios. Instead of building and maintaining separate microservices, teams can embed AI capabilities straight into the integration layer they already use. This reduces development effort, speeds up delivery, and keeps the overall architecture simpler.

Key benefits

  • Faster AI adoption
  • Intelligent and dynamic iFlows can be build
  • Flexibility through AI Core
  • Simplifies architecture with no middleware

Why it makes a difference

This matters because the AI Adapter changes how integrations are built and used. Rather than creating separate services or complex workarounds, teams can add large language model capabilities directly into their existing integrations. This cuts down development effort, speeds things up, and opens the door to smarter and more flexible automation.

AI Adapter availability and Documentation

The AI Adapter is available through SAP Business Accelerator Hub and can be consumed like other standard integration adapters. Its availability depends on the SAP Integration Suite service plan you are using, as not all plans support the same feature set. SAP provides further details in below SAP Notes which outline the supported capabilities across different service plans:

2903776 - SAP Integration Suite – Service Plans and Upgrade Paths - SAP for Me

3188446 - SAP Integration Suite Adapters on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub - SAP for Me

For more details on the adapter architecture, installation steps, configuration options, and documentation, you can refer to the official SAP documentation and resources at the links below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/ai-adapter?locale=en-US
https://api.sap.com/odata/1.0/catalog.svc/Files('6a7cc59b207e4eb384f3af502f8b503f')/$value

Conclusion

With the AI Adapter, SAP is clearly taking Integration Suite beyond traditional connectivity and orchestration. Integration is no longer just about moving data from point A to point B. It can now understand context, respond to changing situations, and deliver smarter, more meaningful outcomes.

For organisations looking to get more value from their data flows, whether that is customer engagement, document processing, real time insights, or flexible workflows, this opens up a whole new set of possibilities. If you are already using SAP BTP and Integration Suite, now is a great time to explore how AI powered integration can enhance your landscape in a very practical way.

entry.title: SAP Business AI & Sustainability: WHY
entry.content:
How AI Powers Sustainable Business Transformation

Sustainability is no longer optional—it’s a business imperative. With over 600 global sustainability reporting regulations and growing stakeholder demands, companies must act now. AI is a game-changer, helping businesses turn sustainability challenges into opportunities.

AI’s Impact on Sustainability

  • Emissions Reduction: AI can cut global greenhouse gas emissions by 5-10% by 2030, supporting the 1.5°C climate goal.
  • Healthcare Advancements: AI improves cancer diagnosis accuracy by up to 36.1%, enhancing patient outcomes.
  • Food Waste Prevention: AI-driven inventory optimization could prevent 907,372 tons of food waste annually, saving $2 billion.

How Executives Can Leverage AI

  • CFOs: Automate ESG reporting and risk management for compliance and cost savings.
  • COOs: Optimize supply chains for lower emissions and waste.
  • CPOs: Screen suppliers for ESG compliance and sustainable sourcing.
  • CSOs: Track carbon footprints and embed sustainability across the business.

Overcoming Sustainability Challenges Despite its potential, sustainability efforts face hurdles:

  • Data Silos: Fragmented data slows decision-making.
  • Manual Processes: Inefficient workflows increase errors.
  • Complex Insights: Distributed data makes actionable insights hard to find.

AI helps by automating data consolidation, improving accuracy, and providing real-time insights—freeing teams to focus on impact.

The Bottom Line AI isn’t just a tool—it’s a catalyst for sustainable growth. By integrating AI into sustainability strategies, businesses can drive efficiency, compliance, and positive global impact.

entry.title: Partner‑Driven Innovation Takes Off with Agent Builder in Joule Studio
entry.content:
In 2025, SAP expanded the Joule ecosystem with Joule Studio, enabling partners to extend Joule through custom skills and autonomous agents. Earlier initiatives demonstrated how partners could build Joule skills to handle targeted workflows and augment enterprise processes, as showcased in the Hack2Build custom Joule skills edition . 

With the Agent Builder now generally available, (read the announcement blog here), SAP has taken a major step forward. Partners can use agentic AI to design custom AI agents capable of planning, reasoning, and executing multi‑step processes across SAP and non‑SAP systems, supported by capabilities such as MCP‑based integrations, multi‑agent orchestration, and business‑context grounding. 

Against this backdrop, the Hack2Build on Custom AI Agents invited partners to explore these capabilities hands‑on for this inaugural edition. 

 

Hack2Build Custom AI Agents Edition 

The Hack2Build Custom AI Agents Edition focused on a select group of early‑adopter partners who received hands‑on coaching from SAP experts to work with the newly released Agent Builder capability. SAP Product and solution architecture specialists supported partners in shaping their designs and aligning them with specific customer needs.

SAP Partners participated in Hack2Build custom AI Agents first edition- January 2026

 Partners overcame early product‑maturity challenges and succeeded in building functional custom AI agents in less than seven days during the hack phase. The initiative culminated in a Live Demo Jam, where all teams presented their working prototypes to a panel of SAP judges.

This was the first Hack2Build where partners worked directly with agentic AI tooling, experimenting with autonomous reasoning, multi‑agent orchestration, system integration patterns, and MCP‑enabled workflows.

🏆Winners

The winning solutions stood out for addressing customer‑critical challenges while thoughtfully complementing SAP’s portfolio. With creative use of agentic AI, the teams demonstrated how autonomous agents can be embedded into real workflows—connecting systems, reasoning over business data, and driving outcomes that matter to customers.

🥇Winner — LTIMindtree: Treasury Auto Ops

Treasury Auto Ops applies agentic AI to transform treasury management with real‑time forecasting, FX exposure assessment, liquidity oversight, and automated hedging recommendations. The agent synthesizes market indicators, macroeconomic signals, and internal treasury data to reach high forecasting accuracy and proactively execute SAP Treasury actions with policy‑compliant governance.
Partners selected for Hack2Build custom AI Agents first edition- January 2026

🥈1st Runner‑Up — Avvale: Early Payment Assistant

Avvale's agent identifies early‑payment discount opportunities across supplier invoices and provides optimized payment recommendations. By balancing liquidity needs with discount potential, it improves working capital, strengthens supplier relationships, and delivers measurable ROI.
Avvale: Early Payment Assistant

 🥉 2nd Runner‑Up — Talan: TALK2BUY

TALK2BUY brings conversational procurement to SAP Cloud ERP. Users can speak or type purchase requisitions in natural language, while the agent enforces procurement policy, recommends compliant items, and sends Microsoft Teams reminders to ensure timely goods‑receipt updates.

Talan: TALK2BUY

 ⭐ Finalists: High‑Quality Solutions from All Participating Partners

  • All for One – Service preparation, escalation & ticket automation agent
  • Capgemini – Smart Vendor Agent to assess supplier data consistency 
  • cbs  – ARIA: Agentic Rate & Inforecord Automation agent
  • DXC Technology – AI Engineering Proposal Assistant (JAEPA)
  • KPMG – Inventory Reconciliation Agent
  • Numen – Financial Performance Advisor
  • NTT Data – FraudCheck Agent
  • Sodales – Job Accommodation & Return‑to‑Work program generator

Special Thanks

A sincere thank you to all participating partners, judges Pavel Penaz, Jan Schneider, Stefan Koenig, Boris Andree, the SAP Build and Joule Studio product teams, and the wider SAP Partner Ecosystem Success units for their support throughout the hack phase.

What’s Next?

The Hack2Build initiative now moves into the BUILD phase, where all participating partners will work over the next 4–5 weeks to realize the full scope of their agentic solutions. Coached by SAP experts, this phase focuses on maturing designs, enriching integrations, and preparing customer‑ready outcomes based on real business requirements and partner domain expertise.

For partners  interested in joining future Hack2Build, registration for the next edition in March is already open.

entry.title: How AI Automation Eliminates Master Data Duplicates in SAP
entry.content:
The Business Case

Whether running SAP ECC or S/4HANA, most organizations struggle with duplicate master data for materials, vendors, and customers. This isn't just a "clutter" problem, poor material master data leads to users creating Purchase Requisitions with text items, bypassing procurement controls and ruining spend analytics.

Let’s explore how AI automation and data vectorization can clean up the core and prevent duplicates before they happen.

The Engine: What is Data Vectorization?

At Skybuffer, we are constantly exploring the benefits of SAP data vectorization. Simply put, vectorization converts data into numerical arrays (vectors) using embedding functions.

Unlike standard SAP keyword searches, vectors are language-independent and allow algorithms to perform similarity searches. While standard tables see rows and columns, a vectorized model sees the relationship between data points.

How Does It Help?

When data is vectorized, we can execute hybrid searches that are indifferent to word order or exact spelling.

Example: Imagine searching for a global customer like "Skybuffer." You might have different accounts for Poland and Norway. If a user tries to create a new record, they can simply ask:

"Do we have a Customer master record for Skybuffer in Poland?"

By querying vectorized KNA1 table data, the AI identifies the record even if the naming convention differs slightly.

 Pic.1 Hybrid Search based on Vectorized KNA1 Table Data

Real-World Integration Points

Hybrid search is most powerful when embedded directly into the SAP business process:

  • Preventative Checks: During the "Save" action in SAP, a custom check against vectorized data can alert the user to a potential duplicate.

  • External Access: Quick searches via Microsoft Teams or SAP Fiori to find existing masters on the go.

  • Smart Procurement: Identifying an existing Material Master during the ordering process, preventing the user from resorting to a "text item."

 Pic.2 Quick Searches of SAP Master Data based on Vectorized Data

Conclusion

Generative AI is a powerhouse when used wisely. Many companies struggle to find the right use cases, often just mirroring existing SAP processes in a chat window. That isn’t true AI automation.

Our approach is different: we focus on the gaps in standard automation. By using AI to simplify data discovery, we reduce development effort and provide an intelligent, frictionless experience for SAP users.

Closing Statement

Keep automating. Remember: smart customization is a competitive advantage, not a deviation from the standard. Build with AI in mind to slash support costs and maximize your ROI via the Skybuffer AI platform.

entry.title: JSON vs TOON: Experimenting with in LLM-Optimized Data Formats
entry.content:
Introduction

At some point in every LLM project, we do the same thing: we take a dataset, serialize it as JSON, paste it into a prompt, and quietly hope the model “figures it out”.

For small samples, it usually does.

But as the data grows, things get fuzzy.

JSON has become the default data input format for LLM prompts, not because it is optimal, but because it is familiar. It is deeply embedded in APIs, storage systems, and validation pipelines. It is readable, ubiquitous, and already part of almost every data workflow.  JSON is structured, explicit, and easy to understand (readable), which explains why it became the default in the first place.

However, LLMs do not parse JSON the way traditional programs do. They tokenize it. That distinction matters because every repeated key, quote, and brace consumes context and costs without necessarily adding semantic value for the model.

While JSON remains the universal standard for APIs, a new contender, "TOON," has emerged to specifically address the token-heavy nature of JSON in AI workflows.

TOON is an emerging serialization format designed for LLM prompts. Its primary goal is token efficiency: minimizing the number of tokens required to represent structured data without losing schema information. TOON enters this space with a narrow but important claim: structured data can be represented in a way that is cheaper and easier for LLMs to process, even if it is less ideal for traditional software tooling.

This article does not argue that TOON should replace JSON.

Instead, it asks a simpler and more practical question:

What actually changes when we feed the same task and the same data to an LLM using JSON versus TOON?

To answer this, I ran a small experiment to examine three things: whether LLMs can correctly interpret TOON, how much token usage changes, and how the input format affects latency.


The Experiment


To measure both data formats fairly, the dataset, the initial prompt, and the user tasks were kept exactly the same. The only variable was the data format itself. 

  • For the dataset, I used Python’s Faker library to generate 300 rows of product data. The same data was then serialized into both JSON and TOON. This is what the first row looks like
    • JSON
{
    "id": 1,
    "uuid": "b4a1a3a9-944b-48e9-9f59-3e0409b56e3e",
    "sku": "EDN-40866175",
    "name": "TechCorp Classic Board Game",
    "category": "Toys",
    "description": "Air service station tree gas individual factor. Science know it whether return some happen executive. Cut my treatment sometimes.",
    "price": 1690.82,
    "currency": "USD",
    "stock": 422,
    "manufacturer": "SmartBrand",
    "barcode": "0649893317279",
    "weight_kg": 32.96,
    "rating": 2.7,
    "reviews": 4818,
    "is_active": true,
    "created_at": "2025-12-29T14:12:29.674107"
  }​
  • TOON
id: 1
uuid: b4a1a3a9-944b-48e9-9f59-3e0409b56e3e
sku: EDN-40866175
name: TechCorp Classic Board Game
category: Toys
description: Air service station tree gas individual factor. Science know it whether return some happen executive. Cut my treatment sometimes.
price: 1690.82
currency: USD
stock: 422
manufacturer: SmartBrand
barcode: 0649893317279
weight_kg: 32.96
rating: 2.7
reviews: 4818
is_active: true
created_at: "2025-12-29T14:12:29.674107"​
  • Three tasks were used to evaluate the formats, which are:
    • What is the price of the product with id 42? (a lookup task)
    • What are all the IDs of products that are phones? (a filtering task)
    • Return all the data for the product with id 121. (a record retrieval task)
  • Other settings: gpt 4o model (default settings), latency was measured using the Python time library before and after the API call, and only the input tokens were counted using tiktoken (o200k_base encoding)


To reduce randomness, I ran 10 trials per format (30 queries each format: 10 × 3 tasks). Execution order was alternated to reduce the chance that the transient API load influenced only one format.

The Results

From the results above, a few clear observations emerged.

In terms of accuracy, both JSON and TOON returned correct results across all tasks. This indicates that the LLM was able to correctly interpret the TOON structure and extract the required information just as reliably as it did with JSON.

In terms of token count, TOON used approximately 22 percent fewer tokens than JSON for the same dataset. This reduction came from eliminating repeated structural elements present in JSON.

Latency showed a similar pattern. On average, prompts using JSON took around 36 seconds to complete, while the TOON version took approximately 22 seconds. This represents roughly a 36 percent improvement in runtime. When normalized, this corresponds to about 0.50 seconds per 1,000 tokens for TOON, compared to 0.61 seconds per 1,000 tokens for JSON.

Conclusion

These results are not conclusive proof that TOON is better than JSON in all cases. JSON structures can become deeply complex, and it is unclear how far TOON can scale before readability or model comprehension becomes an issue. It is also worth noting that LLMs have mostly been trained on significantly more JSON formats than TOONs.

What this experiment suggests is not a replacement of JSON with TOON, but a more nuanced takeaway: TOON can be a promising alternative at the prompt layer, particularly when mid-sized structured datasets need to be injected into LLM prompts and token optimization, or speed is a concern.

JSON remains the right choice for systems and tooling. TOON may be a useful option for models.

entry.title: Human First AI: A Mindset for the Future
entry.content:
Human First AI: A Mindset for the Future

We need to learn how to use AI, or so the message goes everywhere. There's no shortage of courses tutorials, best practices, and frameworks out there. But almost no one is talking about how we should think about AI, or what we should pay attention to when we use it.

That gap matters more than we realize.

 

The Gap Between Progress and Uncertainty

From the outside, everything looks positive. Productivity is up, outputs arrive faster and asks that used to take hours now take minutes. It looks like we're making real progress.

But when you talk to people actually using AI at work, you hear something different. Many say they feel less certain about their own thinking. They hesitate more than before. They move faster but often with less confidence about the direction they're heading.

AI does more than help us complete tasks. It changes how we think, how we form decisions, and how responsibility gets distributed across teams. Once you start noticing that, you can't really unsee it.

 

AI Doesn't Stay in the Background

When AI started showing up at work, we talked about it using familiar language: rollout, training, adoption, enablement. That framing made sense because those are the words we've used when talking about new software for years.

But when you listen to how people actually describe their experience with AI, they use different words. They talk about conversations they had with it. Suggestions it made. Answers to complicated questions that were generated in record time.

The language has changed, and that tells us something.

Most tools we're familiar with behave predictably. You give an instruction, the program executes and you get a result. AI behaves differently because it responds according to context, fills in gaps you didn't specify, and anticipates what might come next. Because it works through language, it sits very close to how we humans think and reason.

You can imagine how some everyday work situations might play out: specifications written by AI, reviewed by AI, and approved by humans who skim them because everything sounds reasonable. Meeting summaries generated for meetings where half the participants were multitasking. Answers that feel complete enough that no one knows quite where to push back.

Nothing here looks broken, and most of it looks efficient, which is exactly why the change is easy to miss.

Decisions are made differently. Questions that would normally be debated for a long time don't stay open as long. Uncertainty disappears earlier in the process. Discussions get shorter, not because people don't care but because a usable answer arrived and removed the pressure to think together.

None of this means people are doing something wrong. It just means that AI doesn't stay in the background the way earlier tools did. It participates in the thinking process.

 

When AI Starts Acting on Its Own

We're moving from AI that assists to AI that acts. Not in a science fiction way, but in very practical everyday workflows.

AI assistants respond when we ask them something, but agents are able to go further. They can plan steps, make choices, and perform actions. They work toward a goal rather than responding to a single prompt.

With traditional software tools, humans stay in charge. You decide what matters, when to act, and when to stop. With agents, part of that authority moves into the system. You define an objective, and the system decides how to move forward with it. It tracks progress and decides when something is done. Often it does this while you're focused on something else.

This is what we usually call delegation, and delegation can be useful. But delegating to agents only works when someone remains aware of what's happening and is prepared to step in if needed.

With agents, that awareness is harder to maintain. The system is working while you're doing other things and it completes tasks without checking in. Over time, you might stop paying close attention. The work gets done and the outcome exists, but ownership becomes harder to locate.

When AI acts, the key skill becomes oversight, interpretation, and knowing when to intervene. That means paying attention to what decisions the system is making along the way, what assumptions it's making, and what no one is actively looking at anymore.

 

The Right Human in the Right Loop

We hear a lot about the importance of having a human in the loop. It's often presented as the safety net, as if as long as a human is there, everything will be fine. But here's the question that rarely gets discussed: is it the right human in the right loop?

A human who is only there to click through a checklist is not a safeguard. A human needs the right technical knowledge, context, time to think, and the authority to say no. Real safety requires more than just human presence. Otherwise "human in the loop" becomes a checkbox, and we only focus on the answer rather than asking if it solved the right problem.

 

Mindset as the Missing Layer

Most of the challenges people experience with AI aren't technical. They come from assumptions we bring into the interaction before we even open a tool.

Mindset shows up in what we expect the system to do for us, what we decide is no longer our job, and what we stop questioning because the output sounds confident. These choices might feel small, but they can accumulate over time.

There are three key tradeoffs that mindset makes visible:

Speed. AI helps us move faster, and speed usually gets treated as an obvious benefit. At the same time, speed changes how judgment works. When we don't have time to sexperience uncertainty or reconsider the question itself, decisions can form before the problem is fully understood.

Efficiency. Efficient systems remove friction, which is often the goal. But friction is also where learning happens. Explaining something, revisiting a decision, or struggling with a problem are moments where understanding deepens. When those moments disappear, work still moves forward but the understanding behind the work becomes weaker.

Confidence. AI outputs often sound sure of themselves, even when the answer depends heavily on context. Confidence used to come from spending time with a problem, struggling with it, and only gradually understanding it well enough to have an opinion about it. Now confidence can arrive already packaged, written in fluent language that sounds convincing even when no one has fully worked through the details.

These three areas affect how people learn, how they trust, and how they imagine possibilities. When we can't see how decisions were made, we trust the system instead of other people. Then when something goes wrong, no one knows who's responsible. When the next step is always suggested, fewer alternatives get explored.

 

Practical Guidance

Can vs. Should

There's a lot that AI can do. But "can" and "should" are very different questions.

Some uses are clever in theory but questionable in practice. Things like automating personal messages, sending AI-written feedback you didn't actually edit or read, or making decisions about people without understanding their situation.

Here's a simple example: if I let AI write all my updates or emails for me, it might save time and remove an annoying task from my day. But something else might happen too. My cognitive abilities that I use for writing will start to weaken over time as writing is a form of thinking. What I'd be missing isn't just the words, but the clarity behind my thinking and the reasoning behind my arguments.

It's tempting to automate first and think later, but we need to be able to pause and ask whether something is actually wise or responsible, even if we can get some gains in efficiency with automating it.

 

Three Questions to Stay Intentional

Before you use AI for any task, pause for just a moment and ask yourself:

What am I handing over?

What am I keeping?

What do I need to know before trusting the output?

That small pause keeps you oriented and intentional rather than just letting AI happen to you.

These questions take seconds, but they build the foundation for working with AI deliberately. They help you notice when you're about to automate something you actually need to understand, or when you're trusting output without knowing enough about how it was generated.

 

What Human First Actually Means

Many organizations today talk about being AI first, and that's a good thing. It has helped people move from talking to doing. and it has given us permission to experiment and learn in practice with AI.

What I'm interested in is what needs to exist alongside AI first as these systems become more capable.

Human first means being deliberate about where we stay involved. It means recognizing which parts of the work affect understanding, trust, and responsibility, and choosing not to step away from those parts simply because a system can handle them faster.

Rejecting automation is not smart, but we need to notice what automation changes. Saving time is valuable, but losing awareness has a cost, even when the effects are not immediately visible.

The goal is to stay intentional. To notice where speed and efficiency help and where they remove something important. To notice when our confidence comes from real understanding versus just accepting what the AI produced.

If we only focus on learning the technical side and the latest features, we'll always feel like we're behind because the technology is developing so fast. But if we develop a mindset to approach AI, that foundation won't change even as the technology does. That's what will keep us grounded.

AI will keep evolving whether we reflect on it or not. What remains our responsibility is how we work with it. 

That's what Human First AI means. Staying involved where judgment matters and being responsible for the decisions we make with AI.

 

This post is based on a talk I gave at SAP AI Ambassador Network's AI Academy on January 29th. The presentation explored how AI changes the way we think and make decisions at work, and what it means to develop an intentional approach to using these systems.

entry.title: How SAP CX AI Toolkit Integrations Create Real Business Value
entry.content:
The SAP CX AI Toolkit becomes most powerful when connected to the everyday systems your teams rely on: email, CRM, commerce data, and enterprise documents. These integrations allow the AI to give timely insights, automate prep work, and improve customer interaction.

Below is a concise, business-friendly snapshot of what each integration delivers.

📧Microsoft 365

Turn emails, meetings, and files into instant insights
The toolkit can use Outlook, calendar events, and OneDrive files to help employees stay informed. 

Business value:

  • Sales gets a quick briefing before meetings: summaries of email threads, open items, and customer tone.
  • Support agents can quickly understand long email exchanges without reading everything.
  • Managers receive helpful daily digests highlighting risks or important follow-ups.

👥SAP Customer Data Platform (CDP)

See the customer as one unified profile
CDP data helps teams deliver more relevant and personalized customer experiences. 

Business value:

  • Marketing can generate targeted messages based on real customer behavior.
  • Sales reps instantly understand a prospect’s recent activity, interests, and engagement.
  • Customer success teams detect early churn signals.

🤝SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C)

Smarter sales and service actions using CRM data
C4C integration brings account, contact, and case details directly into AI workflows. 

Business value:

  • Sales gets one-click account summaries.
  • Service agents receive recommended next steps while viewing a case.

🛠SAP Sales & Service Cloud v2

Next-level CRM intelligence
This integration enriches AI suggestions with modern CRM data.

Business value:

  • Service teams get proactive recommendations based on past issues.
  • Sales leaders improve forecast accuracy with AI‑surfaced engagement patterns.

🛒SAP Commerce Cloud

Bring commerce activity into conversations
Commerce Cloud provides product and customer interaction data for AI-driven assistance. 

Business value:

  • Chatbots help shoppers with product questions and cross‑sell suggestions.
  • Merchandising sees which products customers ask about or struggle with.
  • Sales reps know what a customer browsed before calling.

📦Azure Blob Storage

Tap into your company’s document knowledge
Azure storage feeds additional documents into the AI‑powered Q&A engine.

Business value:

  • Field teams get instant answers from manuals or specs.
  • Product managers turn long documents into short summaries.

🔍Entity Matching

AI that recognizes your business IDs automatically
Rules allow AI to recognize references like “order 5531” or “case 1023” in messages. 

Business value:

  • Service agents jump straight to the right record without searching.
  • Sales reps get context instantly when documents or emails reference deal IDs.

✔ In Short

By connecting your most important business systems, the #SAP CX AI Toolkit becomes a practical, everyday assistant that

  • Prepares your teams faster
  • Reduces busywork
  • Improves customer interactions
  • Surfaces insights you’d otherwise miss

More info

Suggested learning.sap.com resources and what to look for:

  • Starting with AI in SAP Customer Experience Solutions 
  • Utilizing SAP CX AI Toolkit for SAP Commerce Cloud 
  • SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 - CX AI Toolkit, Gen AI & Machine Learning 
  • Building AI Tools and Agents for SAP CX Solutions 

Official product documentation

  • Setting Up your SAP CX AI Toolkit System

entry.title: New Espresso Session: Introducing the Utilities Customer Self-Service Agent on February 19
entry.content:
Dear Utilities Community, 

Discover the newly released Utilities Customer Self-Service Agent, a groundbreaking solution designed to address end-customer needs through human-like and effective interactions, to deliver 24/7 support, reduce service costs, and strengthen customer relationships.

This AI-powered agent integrates seamlessly with SAP S/4HANA Utilities, enabling personalized customer experiences with full context awareness, covering contracts, tariffs, products, consumption, and in future even up-selling opportunities and other proactive engagements.

 

 

What You’ll Learn in This Session:

  • Solution Overview: Explore core capabilities and integration with SAP S/4HANA Utilities—and see how it transforms customer engagement.
  • Demo: Experience a hands-on demo of the agent and discover its powerful features in action.
  • Implementation Insights: Gain expert guidance on technical requirements and strategies for a smooth, successful rollout.

Why Should Join?

This session is designed for utility companies, customer service managers, IT leads, and SAP solution architects who want to enhance customer engagement and optimize service operations.

Register here and join Bruno Pincolini in this session.

entry.title: When Innovation Meets Real Business Impact - VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026
entry.content:
A community powered by creativity

The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon has once again shown what happens when passionate people, real business challenges, and SAP Business Technology Platform come together. Teams of customers, partners, and experts turned ideas into tangible solutions that demonstrate how SAP BTP can drive measurable value in day-to-day operations.​

Over the course of the Hackathon, every team brought its own perspective, business context and technical skills – from integration and automation to SAP Business AI and extensions on SAP BTP.

The result: a set of working prototypes that not only impress technically, but are rooted in real customer needs and adoption potential.

Celebrating the 2026 finalists

After intense jury deliberation, three teams earned a spot in the grand final at SYNC 2026 for their strong presentations, clear business impact, and high potential for real-world rollout.​

  • Promocean (supported by SOA People) – “Operational Excellence through SAP Business AI”: A showcase of how SAP BTP and Business AI can streamline operations and enhance decision-making in a very pragmatic way.​

  • Ecotone (supported by Expertum) – “Interface Maintenance”: A smart solution addressing the often underestimated pain of interface management, using SAP BTP to bring transparency, stability and control.​

  • NTT Data – “ProTrack”: A forward-looking use case that leverages SAP BTP to better track, steer, or optimize processes​

These finalists did not just deliver “cool demos”, they delivered stories where business and technology reinforce each other, with SAP BTP at the core as the innovation platform.

A strong field beyond the finalists

Of course, a hackathon is about much more than just three winners. Aarini Consulting, Nederlandse Spoorwegen, and Simac IT & BSC also delivered impressive solutions that made the jury’s job anything but easy.​

Each of these teams tackled real business scenarios and used SAP BTP services to create innovation that can move quickly from prototype to production. The organizers explicitly encourage all teams to work with their coaches on post-hackathon follow-up to drive implementation and adoption, because the real success of a hackathon is measured when the ideas go live.

SYNC 2026: where the stage is yours

As recognition of this milestone, the three finalist teams are invited to present at SYNC 2026, the VNSG Annual Conference on March 12. During the opening session, each finalist will pitch their solution live on stage to the SYNC audience. The audience will then vote to determine the VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon Champion – a great opportunity to showcase innovation, customer impact and the power of SAP BTP in front of the wider SAP community.

Keep the momentum going

For everyone involved – finalists, other teams, coaches and the broader community – this hackathon is a starting point, not an ending. The next steps are about refining solutions, planning production rollouts, and scaling adoption so that the innovation created during the hackathon translates into lasting business impact.​

If you are part of the VNSG or wider SAP community and feel inspired by these stories, take this as a call to action: bring your own business challenges, experiment on SAP BTP and co-innovate with partners and customers.

The SYNC 2026 VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon has proven once more that with the right mix of creativity, collaboration and platform capabilities, you can turn ideas into value – fast...

SAP Business Technology Platform SAP Build SAP Integration Suite

@hansvp @qmrjvd @sricsi98 @BvE @mdschoenmakers @tamasszirtes @f_van_leeuwen @Petra1 @BartvdKamp @tedcastelijns @Arjan_deMol @dvvelzen @JdTeuling @AnnikaHeus @LaurensSteffers

entry.title: Asset Management in SAP Cloud ERP 2602
entry.content:
Welcome to the blog post for asset management in SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release! This release introduces significant innovations in maintenance management, designed to enhance productivity for maintenance experts, improve safety and compliance protocols, and provide more actionable, centralized data.

Below, you’ll find a curated selection of release highlights from our core product management team. Before diving in, I invite you to watch the release highlights video, featuring a digital version of me! 😀

 

Table of Contents

New Asset Overview SAP Fiori app (+demo) 
Permit to work: new scope item (7Z4) (+demo) 
Enhancements to the Manage Maintenance Notification app (+demo) 
Further innovations 
SAP Asset Performance Management 
More information/additional links

New Asset Overview SAP Fiori app

To address the challenge of navigating multiple screens to gather asset information, the 2602 release introduces the Asset Overview app. This SAP Fiori application provides a single, unified interface for maintenance experts to view and interact with the complete hierarchy of their enterprise assets. Access to this application requires EAM - Asset Overview (SAP_EAM_BC_AOVW_MNG_PC) business catalog, which is included in role templates such as Maintenance Planner (SAP_BR_MAINTENANCE_PLANNER), Maintenance Technician (SAP_BR_MAINTENANCE_TECHNICIAN), and Maintenance Supervisor (SAP_BR_MAINTENANCE_SUPERVISOR).

Key features and capabilities:

Unified hierarchy visualization: The app offers a comprehensive top-down and bottom-up view of your asset structure, combining functional locations and the equipment installed within them into a single, traversable tree. Users can expand individual nodes or the entire hierarchy to its lowest level with a single click. A powerful search function allows users to isolate a specific asset and display its complete branch up to the top-level parent.

The new app aims to provide asset-centric data access, focused on:

  • Flexible column layout: This feature allows a user to select an asset in the hierarchy on the left-hand panel and view its detailed information on the right-hand panel without leaving the main screen.
  • Master and transactional data: The detail view displays crucial master data, such as manufacturer and model number, alongside visual indicators for warranty status (e.g., color-coded for active, expired or not maintained). It also provides direct access to related transactional data, including open maintenance notifications and orders associated with the asset.

Integrated maintenance actions: The app is not just for viewing data; it enables immediate action. Users can create maintenance notifications directly from the asset detail page through two methods:

  • Quick create: A simple form requiring a description to generate a notification.
  • Joule, SAP’s AI Copilot: Users can leverage the integrated digital assistant, Joule, to create a notification using natural language prompts without leaving the app.

Learn more watching the demo below:

 

Permit to work: new scope item (7Z4)

The 2602 release introduces the Permit to Work functionality under scope item 7Z4. This feature provides a standardized process for issuing permits for high-risk maintenance activities, ensuring work is performed in a safe, secure, and compliant manner. It is particularly critical for industries such as manufacturing, oil and gas, mining, and utilities.

At its core, the Permit to Work process serves as a formal authorization system. It's designed to foster close collaboration between your maintenance, operations, and safety teams, ensuring everyone is aligned on the required precautions to minimize risks.

Initially, this scope item is available for Germany and the US. Availability for other countries will follow in subsequent updates, with a target for all standard countries by the 2702 release.

Prerequisite scope items include Reactive Maintenance (4HH), Proactive Maintenance (4H), Operational and Overhead Maintenance (4BT), and Maintenance Resource Scheduling (4WM).

Core components and configuration

New applications: This functionality is delivered with two new SAP Fiori apps:

  • Manage Work Permit Template (F4692): Allows for the creation of reusable templates with pre-defined information. This reduces the time required to create new permits and ensures that minimum safety and data standards are consistently met.
  • Manage Work Permits (F6579): The primary application for creating, processing, and managing the lifecycle of individual work permits. Permits can be created with reference to a maintenance order, an existing permit, or a pre-configured template.

Process overview

1. Initiation: A planner identifies a maintenance activity that requires a permit and initiates its creation, selecting a permit type such as hot work, cold work, or confined space.
2. Approval: The permit is submitted for review. In the initial 2602 release, the permit is automatically set to a "Ready for Issue" status. Flexible workflow capabilities are planned for future releases.
3. Issuance: An authorized user, such as a supervisor, issues the permit to the designated technician.
4. Execution and return: The technician completes the work and returns the permit to the issuer.
5. Closure: The issuer verifies the work site, normalizes the technical object, and formally closes the permit in the system. The system supports actions to "retain and close" or "retain and deactivate" the permit if work needs to be suspended and resumed later.

 Learn more watching the demo below by Ebin Joseph, from product management:

 

Enhancements to the Manage Maintenance Notification app

The Manage Maintenance Notification (F5777) app has been updated with several new features to improve usability and provide deeper insights.

Situation handling: The app now uses situation handling to deliver instant alerts to the maintenance team when a new notification is created or a key status is updated. For example, an alert can be triggered when a notification is automatically generated due to a condition-based maintenance threshold breach, enabling a proactive response.

Advanced filtering: The list report now includes "Asset Location" and "Sort Field" as filter criteria. This allows users to more effectively narrow down the list of notifications to the specific assets or areas for which they are responsible.

Visual charts for measurement documents: For notifications generated from a measurement document threshold breach, the header now includes a chart. This provides a clear visual representation of the measurement readings against their defined thresholds, allowing for quicker analysis.

Reference order tracking: A "Reference Order" field has been added as a filter and a column in the list report. This enables maintenance teams to easily identify and track all notifications that were created as a follow-on work request from a technician in the field.

Conversational AI: Similar to the Asset Overview app, users can now create maintenance notifications directly within this app using natural language conversations with Joule AI Copilot.

 Learn more watching the demo below by Syed Kader Abdul Wahab, from product management:

 

Further innovations

Perform Maintenance Jobs app - Long text formatting: Clarity in work instructions is critical for maintenance technicians. Long text fields for maintenance orders and jobs can now be formatted. This enhancement allows for the use of bolding, bullet points, and other formatting options to structure text, which improves the readability and organization of detailed instructions and notes.

Manage Maintenance Plans app - Deletion of sort fields: To improve data quality and efficiency, the Define Sort Fields for Maintenance Plans configuration activity now includes a delete option. You can now remove outdated or invalid customized sort field values. This ensures that only accurate and relevant sort field values are available in the Maintenance Plan application, improving accuracy in plan management.

Centralized processing of maintenance order workflows in SAP Task Center: Efficiency in approval processes is a key driver of productivity. Maintenance Order workflow attributes are now accessible in SAP Task Center, allowing for centralized processing of approvals. The following attributes are included: Order Description, Order Type, Planner Group, Planning Plant, Basic End Date, Basic Start Date, Priority, and Technical Object. This centralization helps managers and approvers process tasks from a single, unified inbox.

SAP Asset Performance Management 

SAP Asset Performance Management integrates with SAP Cloud ERP and empowers asset owners, plant managers, and reliability engineers to optimize maintenance strategies, measure and improve asset performance, and follow through on your sustainability objectives. The 2601 release highlights include bulk activation and deactivation of system elements, more flexible risk and criticality assessment outcomes, CAP-based reliability assessment applications with extensibility, configurable RCM consequence questionnaires, validity periods for assessments, time-window–based rule processing, and embedded analytics dashboards. Read the complete blog post by Kate Dowle: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2601.

If you’d like to stay up to date with the latest APM innovations, follow my colleague Kate Dowle who regularly shares updates and new blog posts on the topic.
 

More information / additional links 

Find all enablement assets and update blog post for Asset Management: SAP S/4HANA Cloud for Asset Management – The Link Collection

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Also, visit the Asset Management community page. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts. 

Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up-to-date to our latest enablement assets: Fernanda Rodrigues | LinkedIn

 

 Find all our assets

Do you want to learn more about the main innovations in different lines of business in the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release? The release 2602 showcases more 130 innovation highlights across the portfolio. Check out our release document for all the details!

Enablement

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Click on the enablement wheel below and check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you: 

 

 ️ Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series!

From January 12-16, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find the recording and presentation for Asset Management as well as 21 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand – anywhere, anytime.
